title: What a Million Roman Nails Tell Us About Progress
subtitle: 罗马人为什么要埋掉一百万枚钉子？

paragraphs.en: Nearly 2,000 years ago, as the Romans began to pull out of Scotland, they left behind a curious treasure: 10 tons of nails, nearly a million of the things. The nail hoard was discovered in 1960 in a four-metre-deep pit covered by two metres of gravel. Why had the Romans buried a million nails? The likely explanation is that the withdrawal was rushed, and they didn't want the local Caledonians getting their hands on 10 tons of weapon-grade iron.
paragraphs.zh: 大约两千年前，当罗马人开始撤出苏格兰时，他们留下了一件奇特的宝藏：十吨钉子，将近一百万枚。这批钉子窖藏于1960年被发现，位于一个四米深的坑中，上面覆盖着两米厚的砾石。罗马人为何要埋藏一百万枚钉子？可能的解释是撤军仓促，他们不希望当地的喀里多尼亚人得到十吨武器级的铁。

paragraphs.en: Later civilisations would value the skilled blacksmith's labour in a nail even more than the raw material. As Roma Agrawal explains in her new delightful book Nuts and Bolts, early 17th-century Virginians would sometimes burn down their homes if they were planning to relocate. This was an attempt to recover the valuable nails, which could be reused after sifting the ashes. The idea that one might burn down an entire house just to reclaim the nails underlines how scarce, costly and valuable the simple-seeming technology was.
paragraphs.zh: 后来的文明对钉子中熟练铁匠的劳动价值的重视甚至超过了原材料。正如罗马·阿格拉瓦尔在她令人愉快的新书《螺丝与螺母》中所解释的，17世纪初的弗吉尼亚人如果计划搬迁，有时会烧毁自己的房屋。这是为了回收宝贵的钉子，这些钉子可以在筛过灰烬后重新使用。人们会为了回收钉子而烧毁整栋房子，这一想法凸显了这项看似简单的技术曾经是多么稀缺、昂贵和有价值。

paragraphs.en: The price of nails fell by 90% between the late 1700s and mid-1900s, as economist Daniel Sichel points out in a research paper. According to Sichel, although the falling price of nails was driven partly by cheaper iron and cheaper energy, most of the credit goes to nail manufacturers who simply found more efficient ways to turn steel into nails. Nails themselves have changed over the years, but Sichel studied them because they haven't changed much. Roman nails are still clearly nails.
paragraphs.zh: 正如经济学家丹尼尔·西切尔在一篇研究论文中指出的，从18世纪末到20世纪中叶，钉子的价格下降了90%。据西切尔称，尽管钉子价格下跌部分源于更便宜的铁和能源，但大部分功劳归于钉子制造商，他们只是找到了将钢变成钉子的更有效方法。这些年来钉子本身已有变化，但西切尔研究它们正是因为它们变化不大。罗马钉子显然仍然是钉子。

paragraphs.en: I make no apology for being obsessed by a particular feature of these objects: their price. I am an economist, after all. After writing two books about the history of inventions, one thing I've learnt is that while it is the enchantingly sophisticated technologies that get all the hype, it's the cheap technologies that change the world. Solar panels had few niche uses until they became cheap; now they are transforming the global energy system.
paragraphs.zh: 我毫不讳言自己痴迷于这些物品的一个特定特征：它们的价格。毕竟，我是一名经济学家。在写了两本关于发明史的书之后，我学到的一件事是，尽管那些迷人而精密的技术获得了所有炒作，但改变世界的却是廉价的技术。太阳能电池板在变得便宜之前几乎没有小众用途；如今它们正在改变全球能源系统。

words.meaning: adj. 小众的，缝隙市场的
